dominant_error_pattern: Readers conflate the control group (condition without manipulation) with other experimental roles, treating any non-treatment group label as interchangeable rather than recognizing that control group is the absence of the independent variable.
instructional_red_flags: A names the exposed group as experimental (correct naming). B invents 'independent group' terminology that conflates the independent variable with group designation. D applies dependent variable (outcome measurement) to group classification—teach role definitions using concrete example: background music (independent variable) is ON in experimental group, OFF in control group. Dependent variable is the measured outcome (e.g., task performance), not the group.

dominant_error_pattern: Students over-generalize epidemiological facts about ADHD gender distribution while overlooking the school psychologist's specific professional boundary: school psychologists contribute to identification (observation and measurement) but do not diagnose (clinical determination).
instructional_red_flags: A misplaces ADHD prevalence at unrealistically high 17%. B conflates prevalence trends with overdiagnosis in schools. C correctly identifies the professional boundary but is marked incorrect—clarify that school psychologists are key collaborators in ADHD identification (documenting attention deficits via assessment) but medical/clinical diagnosis belongs to physicians. Option D is correct because it is an epidemiological fact; note the distinction between prevalence (how common) and role (who diagnoses).

dominant_error_pattern: Students apply incorrect procedures to the mean calculation, either selecting the highest data point, averaging only central scores, or confusing the median (middle value when sorted) with the arithmetic mean (sum divided by count).
instructional_red_flags: A treats the maximum value (10) as the mean. B averages only the middle two values (5 + 7)/2 = 6, then selects 5.5 as a midpoint error. C applies median procedure (sorts 1, 5, 7, 7, 10; selects middle = 7) instead of summing all—show step-by-step: (1 + 5 + 7 + 7 + 10) ÷ 5 = 30 ÷ 5 = 6.0. Emphasize: mean includes all values; median is the middle value. Both are used in RTI but answer different questions.

dominant_error_pattern: Learners interpret Michele's improved mean (6.0) in isolation rather than anchoring it to her initial baseline (3) and the benchmark for same-aged peers, conflating absolute score with progress trajectory.
instructional_red_flags: A rejects progress without establishing what baseline was or what peer benchmark is. B treats any score below 10 as problematic, ignoring the magnitude of change from baseline. D over-generalizes from RTI involvement to automatic special education referral when RTI is designed to prevent unnecessary referral through intervention—frame interpretation: Michele started at 3, now averages 6; that is +100% improvement from baseline, AND she is approaching peer level. Progress is evident; continue monitoring rather than jumping to special education.
